Timing.evidence: Step 5 (Collateral confirmation) precedes step 6 (Issuance approval) in the documented process sequence. 'This step is now mandatory for every partially-secured guarantee.'

What it checks.evidence: For guarantees not fully covered by an approved facility, the Trade Finance Officer confirms that cash collateral has been received and blocked before issuance can proceed. This step is now mandatory for every partially-secured guarantee.

Legal review with no committed turnaround

Root cause.evidence: Legal review with no committed turnaround; PP-BGID-002 confirms no SLA

Client impact.evidence: clients have no visibility into Legal's queue — cannot plan underlying transaction, RM absorbs escalation calls

Description.evidence: the bespoke-wording journey is the slowest path — clients have no visibility into Legal's queue

Root cause.evidence: Legal team operates without a committed or communicated SLA; no client-facing status interface on the review queue

Client impact.evidence: clients don't know whether collateral has been booked — time-sensitive for underlying contracts with hard guarantee-delivery deadlines

Description.evidence: the collateral-confirmation journey when applicable — clients don't know whether collateral has been booked and the guarantee can therefore be released

Root cause.evidence: collateral blocking is an internal treasury operation with no client-facing notification step

Client impact.evidence: clients are not notified of MT760 acknowledgement — contact beneficiary directly, adding friction at the moment the transaction should be proceeding

Description.evidence: the 'did the beneficiary receive it?' silent gap after MT760 — clients are not notified of MT760 acknowledgement

Root cause.evidence: SWIFT acknowledgement visible to bank's operations team but not relayed to the client — no process step routes it

Definition.evidence: Elicited: count of guarantees issued per calendar month from TFS issuance log; owner Head of Trade Finance; frequency monthly

Current reading.evidence: Elicited: ~40% per FP-BGID-001 and PP-BGID-002 analysis; thresholds green ≥60%, amber 40–59%, red <40%

Definition.evidence: Elicited: % of applications through PS-BGID-001–006 with no exception flag; sourced from workflow audit trail; owner Head of Trade Finance Operations; frequency monthly

Current reading.evidence: Elicited: not currently measured; thresholds green ≤15%, amber 15–25%, red >25%

Definition.evidence: Elicited: % of applications hitting at least one of EX-BGID-001–006; sourced from exception log; owner Trade Finance Manager; frequency monthly

Why it matters.evidence: Elicited: exception rate aggregates PP-BGID-001/002/003 friction and gives early warning before SLA overruns appear in turnaround time reporting

Description.evidence: Applications frequently stall at the credit and facility check when the client has not pre-arranged enough limit.

Root cause.evidence: Applications frequently stall at the credit and facility check when the client has not pre-arranged enough limit.

Description.evidence: clients submitting without contract reference is the biggest recurring issue

What happens.evidence: incomplete app → officer returns it via the portal with a checklist (no defined SLA — usually same day)

Inputs.evidence: Templates live in TFS, version-controlled by Legal

What happens.evidence: The EUR 5M threshold is enforced by TFS — Head of TF must sign-off in-app before issuance proceeds

For guarantees not fully covered by an approved facility, the Trade Finance Officer confirms that cash collateral has been received and blocked before issuance can proceed. This step is now mandatory for every partially-secured guarantee.

How it is met.evidence: https://eur-lex.europa.eu/eli/reg/2024/1624/oj/eng — AMLR obliges institutions to apply CDD at onboarding and on each transaction — fetched 2026-05-26

What it requires.evidence: https://eur-lex.europa.eu/eli/reg/2024/1624/oj/eng — AMLR Article 20: verify identity, beneficial ownership at 25% threshold, screen against sanctions and PEP lists — fetched 2026-05-26

Why it applies.evidence: https://anti-money-laundering.eu/new-customer-due-diligence-cdd-data-points-under-amlr/ — banks are obliged entities; guarantee issuance creates a financial product relationship requiring CDD — fetched 2026-05-26

How it is met.evidence: https://www.tradefinanceglobal.com/guarantees/urdg-758-rules-impact-demand-guarantees/ — standard wording templates and Legal review ensure compliance with URDG terms — fetched 2026-05-26

What it requires.evidence: https://www.tradefinanceglobal.com/guarantees/urdg-758-rules-impact-demand-guarantees/ — guarantee must be in writing; independence principle; bank must pay on first compliant demand — fetched 2026-05-26

Why it applies.evidence: https://iccwbo.org/news-publications/news/icc-demand-guarantee-rules-urdg-758-celebrate-two-years-of-rising-popularity/ — URDG 758 governs demand guarantees where incorporated by reference — fetched 2026-05-26

How it is met.evidence: https://lexparency.org/eu/CRR/ART_111/ — facility utilisation update at PS-BGID-006 records contingent exposure; capital calculation handled by risk management systems — fetched 2026-05-26

What it requires.evidence: https://lexparency.org/eu/CRR/ART_111/ — Article 111: exposure value of off-balance-sheet items = nominal value × credit conversion factor; credit-substitute guarantees assigned 100% CCF — fetched 2026-05-26

Why it applies.evidence: https://lexparency.org/eu/CRR/ART_111/ — each guarantee issued creates a contingent credit exposure that enters the bank's risk-weighted asset calculation — fetched 2026-05-26

How it is met.evidence: https://www.eba.europa.eu/sites/default/files/document_library/Publications/Guidelines/2023/EBA-GL-2023-03/1061654/Guidelines%20ML%20TF%20Risk%20Factors_conslidated.pdf.pdf — CP-BGID-002 sanctions screening addresses sanctions risk factors; broader ML/TF risk assessment to be confirmed with SME — fetched 2026-05-26

What it requires.evidence: https://www.eba.europa.eu/sites/default/files/document_library/Publications/Guidelines/2023/EBA-GL-2023-03/1061654/Guidelines%20ML%20TF%20Risk%20Factors_conslidated.pdf.pdf — obliged entities must assess ML/TF risk factors for trade finance products; enhanced due diligence for cross-border and correspondent banking — fetched 2026-05-26

Why it applies.evidence: https://www.eba.europa.eu/sites/default/files/document_library/Publications/Guidelines/2023/EBA-GL-2023-03/1061654/Guidelines%20ML%20TF%20Risk%20Factors_conslidated.pdf.pdf — trade finance guarantees involve cross-border beneficiaries and complex ownership requiring product-specific risk assessment — fetched 2026-05-26

How it is met.evidence: https://www.bundesbank.de/en/tasks/banking-supervision/legal-basis/national-legal-basis/regulation-governing-large-exposures-and-loans-of-1-million-or-more-grosskredit-und-millionenkreditverordnung--640376 — Germany implements CRR Art. 395 directly; GroMiKV governs national discretions; institutions report large exposures quarterly to BaFin/Bundesbank via COREP LE templates under Art. 394 — fetched 2026-05-26

What it requires.evidence: https://lexparency.org/eu/CRR/ART_395/ — "An institution shall not incur an exposure to a client or group of connected clients the value of which exceeds 25 % of its Tier 1 capital after taking into account the effect of the credit risk mitigation in accordance with Articles 399 to 403" — fetched 2026-05-26

Why it applies.evidence: https://service.betterregulation.com/document/155296 — Part Four (Articles 387–403) applies to "any asset or off-balance sheet item" (Art. 389); each issued bank guarantee is an off-balance-sheet exposure to the applicant client and, via Art. 403 substitution, potentially to the beneficiary guarantor — fetched 2026-05-26

How it is met.evidence: https://www.eba.europa.eu/single-rule-book-qa/qna/view/publicId/2020_5496 — "Mandatory substitution approach according to Article 403 CRR": the protected portion is deducted from the original obligor and added to the guarantor in the COREP LE template; CP-BGID-004 records collateral at issuance, providing the data for this re-attribution — fetched 2026-05-26

What it requires.evidence: https://lexparency.org/eu/CRR/ART_403/ — "Where an exposure to a client is guaranteed by a third party, or is secured by collateral issued by a third party, an institution may treat the part of the exposure which is guaranteed as an exposure incurred on the guarantor and not on the client" — fetched 2026-05-26

Why it applies.evidence: https://www.eba.europa.eu/single-rule-book-qa/qna/view/publicId/2022_6396 — "Mandatory substitution approach according to Articles 401(4) and 403 CRR": when collateral or a counter-guarantee supports a guarantee issued by the bank, the exposure must be re-attributed to the protection provider for large-exposure purposes — fetched 2026-05-26

How it is met.evidence: https://www.betasystems.com/resources/blog/marisk-compliance-through-segregation-of-duties — "MaRisk explicitly requires segregation of duties as a core element of the internal control system" ensuring "incompatible tasks — such as initiating and controlling transactions — are performed by different individuals" — fetched 2026-05-26

What it requires.evidence: https://www.bundesbank.de/resource/blob/623102/bca5bafd72a669115b15c4125e063feb/mL/minimum-requirements-for-risk-management-mindestanforderungen-an-das-risikomanagement-marisk-data.pdf — "A clear organisational segregation of the front office (Markt) and back office (Marktfolge) up to and including management board level" — fetched 2026-05-26

Why it applies.evidence: https://legal.pwc.de/en/news/articles/bafin-publishes-7th-amendment-to-marisk — "Module BTO 1.2 (Loan Processing): addresses requirements for loan origination and monitoring, incorporating EBA Guidelines' supervisory objectives" — fetched 2026-05-26

How it is met.evidence: https://www.betasystems.com/resources/blog/marisk-compliance-through-segregation-of-duties — "Credit operations: Front office and back office separation" cited as the primary application of EBA-aligned segregation requirements in credit processes — fetched 2026-05-26

What it requires.evidence: https://www.bde.es/f/webbde/INF/MenuHorizontal/Normativa/guias/EBA-2021-05-EN.pdf — "measures to manage or mitigate potential conflicts may consist of, among others: i) an appropriate segregation of duties; ii) information barriers" — fetched 2026-05-26

Why it applies.evidence: https://www.eba.europa.eu/activities/single-rulebook/regulatory-activities/internal-governance/guidelines-internal-governance-under-crd — "guidelines apply to all institutions regardless of their governance structures" covering "sound and effective governance arrangements" under CRD — fetched 2026-05-26

How it is met.evidence: https://www.digital-operational-resilience-act.com/Article_6.html — "Financial entities must establish a sound, comprehensive and well-documented ICT risk management framework" — the Trade Finance System that records and issues guarantees must be in scope — fetched 2026-05-26

What it requires.evidence: https://www.digital-operational-resilience-act.com/Article_9.html — "design, procure and implement ICT security policies, procedures, protocols and tools that aim to ensure the resilience, continuity and availability of ICT systems, in particular for those supporting critical or important functions" — fetched 2026-05-26

Why it applies.evidence: https://www.digital-operational-resilience-act.com/Article_5.html — "management body bears the ultimate responsibility for managing the financial entity's ICT risk" and must "define, approve, and oversee the ICT risk management framework" — fetched 2026-05-26

How it is met.evidence: https://kycaml.guide/blog/the-impact-of-gdpr-on-kyc-procedure-a-closer-look/ — "financial institutions typically retain complete KYC files for five years after relationship termination, then delete or anonymise data unless ongoing legal obligations apply" — fetched 2026-05-26

What it requires.evidence: https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/?uri=celex:32016R0679 — Article 6(1)(b)/(c): processing lawful where necessary for contract performance or legal obligation; Article 5(1)(e): personal data kept no longer than necessary; Article 17(3)(b): right to erasure does not apply where retention is required by law — fetched 2026-05-26

Why it applies.evidence: https://gdprlocal.com/gdpr-for-kyc-platforms/ — "Article 6 of GDPR provides lawful bases for processing personal data in KYC platforms, with legal obligation (Article 6(1)(c)) being the primary basis for AML compliance activities such as customer identification" — fetched 2026-05-26

How it is met.evidence: https://anti-money-laundering.eu/new-record-retention-periods-under-art-77-amlr/ — "Regulation (EU) 2024/1624 introduces a fully harmonised and directly applicable EU framework for AML record retention … creating a digitisation obligation for institutions with legacy paper files" — fetched 2026-05-26

What it requires.evidence: https://amlr.eu/article-77-record-retention/ — "Article 77 AMLR requires retention of all CDD records for at least five years after the end of the business relationship … records must be kept in a format that allows machine-readable evaluation" — fetched 2026-05-26

Why it applies.evidence: https://anti-money-laundering.eu/record-keeping-and-retention/ — "Article 40(1)(a) AMLD5 obliges entities to retain a copy of the documents and information which are necessary to comply with the customer due diligence requirements for a period of five years after the end of their business relationship or after the date of an occasional transaction" — fetched 2026-05-26

How it is met.evidence: https://regreportingdesk.com/corep-reporting-errors/ — accurate COREP reporting requires that off-balance-sheet items including guarantees issued are captured with the correct credit conversion factor before submission to the supervisory authority — fetched 2026-05-26

What it requires.evidence: https://www.eba.europa.eu/single-rule-book-qa/qna/view/publicId/2014_1188 — "Template C 07.00 is titled CREDIT AND COUNTERPARTY CREDIT RISKS AND FREE DELIVERIES: STANDARDISED APPROACH TO CAPITAL REQUIREMENTS … covers credit risk in accordance with Chapter 2 of Title II of Part Three of CRR" — fetched 2026-05-26

Why it applies.evidence: https://www.eba.europa.eu/single-rule-book-qa/qna/view/publicId/2014_1055 — "Guarantees received on off-balance sheet exposures … should be reported" under the applicable COREP template; guarantees issued create off-balance-sheet credit exposures requiring supervisory reporting — fetched 2026-05-26

Evidence.evidence: https://www.gtreview.com/supplements/gtr-risk-2025/how-llms-are-transforming-trade-finance-operations/ — "Up to 40% reduction in processing time, depending on document complexity" — fetched 2026-05-26

Relevance.evidence: https://www.gtreview.com/supplements/gtr-risk-2025/how-llms-are-transforming-trade-finance-operations/ — "Advanced models analyse language patterns across documents, identify inconsistencies and flag suspicious activities" — fetched 2026-05-26

The trend.evidence: https://www.gtreview.com/supplements/gtr-risk-2025/how-llms-are-transforming-trade-finance-operations/ — "guarantees and standby letters of credit remain a significant opportunity for transformation ... LLMs can now read, interpret and extract insights at unprecedented speed and accuracy" — fetched 2026-05-26

Evidence.evidence: https://emeraldcapitalco.com/july-global-adoption-of-mletr-are-we-approaching-the-turning-point-for-digital-trade/ — 12 economies have adopted or aligned with MLETR; the 5 major adopters represent nearly 37% of global GDP — fetched 2026-05-26

Relevance.evidence: https://www.tradefinanceglobal.com/posts/status-update-mletr-adoption-in-the-g7-and-emerging-markets/ — "Once we shift towards digitisation, we will have easier compliance, better governance" (Luca Castellani, UNCITRAL) — fetched 2026-05-26

The trend.evidence: https://emeraldcapitalco.com/july-global-adoption-of-mletr-are-we-approaching-the-turning-point-for-digital-trade/ — "The Model Law on Electronic Transferable Records (MLETR) has become a rallying point for digital trade advocacy" and "2024 will go down as a historic turning point" — fetched 2026-05-26

Evidence.evidence: https://www.nomentia.com/blog/digitalize-bank-guarantee-management — enterprises deal with hundreds if not thousands of guarantees yearly against finite facility lines, driving demand for real-time headroom visibility — fetched 2026-05-26

Relevance.evidence: https://www.nomentia.com/blog/digitalized-guarantee-management-in-practice — "Group Treasury can adjust the ideal utilization level and manage the allocation of business among the group's banks" — fetched 2026-05-26

The trend.evidence: https://www.nomentia.com/blog/digitalize-bank-guarantee-management — "Digital solutions centralize all guarantees, automate approval workflows, and provide real-time visibility across guarantee lifecycle" — fetched 2026-05-26

Evidence.evidence: https://www.tradefinanceglobal.com/posts/hsbc-launches-guarantees-digital-portal-for-fi-trade-finance-transactions/ — HSBC's FI Digital Network launched a dedicated guarantee portal with real-time status tracking and automated reporting, subsequently expanded to the full trade product suite — fetched 2026-05-26

Relevance.evidence: https://www.nomentia.com/blog/digitalize-bank-guarantee-management — "use one digital standardized form for all requests and manage entire guarantee lifecycles digitally" — fetched 2026-05-26

The trend.evidence: https://www.tradefinanceglobal.com/posts/hsbc-launches-guarantees-digital-portal-for-fi-trade-finance-transactions/ — "What was always lacking in this space was the ability for banks to automatically check where guarantees are" (Vinay Mendonca, HSBC Chief Growth Officer, Global Trade) — fetched 2026-05-26

Evidence.evidence: https://www.signaturit.com/blog/the-digital-operational-resilience-act-dora/ — DORA mandates ICT incidents be reported to authorities within 24 hours, requires annual continuity testing; entered application January 2025 — fetched 2026-05-26

Relevance.evidence: https://www.eba.europa.eu/activities/direct-supervision-and-oversight/digital-operational-resilience-act — DORA requires banks to maintain robust ICT risk management and continuous supervision of critical third-party digital providers governing guarantee issuance and e-delivery systems — fetched 2026-05-26

The trend.evidence: https://www.signaturit.com/blog/the-digital-operational-resilience-act-dora/ — "DORA Regulation came into force on January 17, 2025" and applies to "more than 20 types" of financial organisations including banks — fetched 2026-05-26
